answerSummary: If you're a newcomer earning under $70,000, a TFSA is usually the better starting point — contributions are flexible, withdrawals are tax-free, and you don't need Canadian income history to benefit. Once your income grows or you have RRSP contribution room from a full year of Canadian income, layering in RRSP contributions makes sense for the tax deduction.
author: Maple Insight Editorial

children.text: A Tax-Free Savings Account (TFSA) is a registered account that lets you invest and save money without paying tax on the growth or withdrawals. You contribute with after-tax dollars — meaning there's no immediate tax deduction — but everything inside the account grows completely tax-free.
As a newcomer, you start accumulating contribution room the year you become a Canadian resident, regardless of your income level. In 2025, the annual limit is

A Registered Retirement Savings Plan (RRSP) is designed for retirement savings. Unlike the TFSA, contributions are tax-deductible — you reduce your taxable income by the amount you contribute. The trade-off: withdrawals in retirement are taxed as regular income.
Your RRSP room is calculated as 18% of your
earned income from the prior tax year — up to a maximum. In your first year in Canada, you'll have little or no RRSP room. Check your Notice of Assessment (NOA) from CRA for your exact amount.
Here's how the two accounts compare on the factors that matter most for newcomers:
TFSA uses after-tax dollars (no deduction). RRSP contributions are tax-deductible.
TFSA growth is completely tax-free. RRSP growth is tax-deferred until withdrawal.
TFSA withdrawals are always tax-free. RRSP withdrawals are taxed as income.
TFSA: $7,000. RRSP: 18% of prior year Canadian income (up to $31,560).
TFSA gives you a full year's room immediately. RRSP room is based on Canadian income only — so very little in year one.
Numbers make this clearer. Here's a real-world scenario for a typical first-year newcomer:

For most newcomers in their first 1–2 years in Canada, the TFSA wins for three reasons: you have contribution room immediately, withdrawals are always tax-free, and the flexibility suits the unpredictability of a new life in a new country.
As your income grows and you accumulate RRSP room, contributing to both makes sense. A common strategy:
TFSA for short- and medium-term goals; RRSP for retirement.
If you're also saving for a home, explore the First Home Savings Account (FHSA) — it combines the best of both worlds for first-time buyers.
Rule of thumb: Open your TFSA the year you arrive. Start your RRSP once you've filed your first Canadian tax return and have confirmed RRSP contribution room.

exampleScenarios.body:
Priya moved to Toronto in January 2025 and earns $58,000/year as a software developer. Her marginal tax rate in Ontario is approximately 29.65%.

TFSA first: She contributes $7,000 to her TFSA. No tax deduction, but all growth is tax-free. She can withdraw any time without penalty.

RRSP room: In 2025, Priya has very little RRSP room because she had no Canadian income in 2024. After filing her 2025 return, she'll have 18% × $58,000 = $10,440 in RRSP room for 2026.

Net result: For Priya's first year, the TFSA is the clear winner — she has room to use immediately and the flexibility to withdraw if needed while settling in.
exampleScenarios.title: Priya, Ontario, $58K income

faqItems.answer: No — TFSA contribution room only accumulates for years in which you are a Canadian resident. If you arrived in 2024, you earn room starting January 1, 2024. Years before your arrival do not count.
faqItems.question: Does my TFSA contribution room accumulate from before I moved to Canada?

faqItems.answer: You can open an RRSP, but your contribution room is based on 18% of your prior year's Canadian earned income. In your very first year (or if you had no Canadian income the year before), you'll have little or no RRSP room. Check your Notice of Assessment (NOA) from CRA for your exact room.
faqItems.question: Can I contribute to an RRSP in my first year with no Canadian income?

faqItems.answer: Yes, absolutely. Many Canadians do both. A common strategy: maximize TFSA first (especially if income is lower), then contribute to RRSP as income and tax rate rise. The FHSA is also worth considering if you plan to buy a home.
faqItems.question: Can I contribute to both a TFSA and an RRSP at the same time?

faqItems.answer: TFSA withdrawals are tax-free at any time, and the room is restored the following January 1. RRSP withdrawals are taxed as income in the year you withdraw — a significant difference. Early RRSP withdrawals (outside the Home Buyers' Plan or Lifelong Learning Plan) are generally discouraged.
faqItems.question: What happens if I withdraw from my TFSA or RRSP?

answerSummary: To receive Canada Child Benefit payments, you must apply — it is not automatic. The fastest way is through CRA My Account or when registering your child's birth. You will need a SIN for yourself and your child, proof of the child's birth, and proof of your immigration status. Processing takes approximately 8 weeks, and you will receive retroactive payments from your eligibility date.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Method 1: Online via CRA My Account (Recommended)
If you already have a CRA My Account set up (see our guide on [registering for CRA My Account](/articles/how-to-register-for-cra-my-account)), this is the fastest and simplest method.
Log in to CRA My Account at canada.ca/cra-my-account
Confirm your personal information (address, marital status)
Add each child you are applying for with their date of birth and SIN
The CRA will process your application and contact you if any additional documents are needed. First payment typically arrives within 8 weeks.
If your child is born in Canada, most provinces have a
that lets you apply for the child's SIN and the CCB at the same time as registering the birth. The hospital provides the necessary forms.
This is the easiest way for parents of newborns — you handle everything in one step.
If you cannot use CRA My Account or the birth registration service, you can apply by completing Form RC66 (Canada Child Benefits Application) and mailing it to your nearest tax centre.
RC66SCH (Annex A) — Immigration Status Confirmation form (required for newcomers who are not Canadian citizens)
Proof of the child's birth (see below)
Proof of your immigration status (see below)
Identity and immigration status — one of:
Work Permit (if temporary resident applying after 18 months)
Proof of date of birth — one of:
Hospital birth record or birth certificate
If your child was born outside Canada, you will need an official birth certificate, possibly with a certified translation if it is not in English or French.
Apply for your child's SIN at a Service Canada Centre. Bring the child's birth certificate and your own ID. A child's SIN is needed for the CCB application and for future RESP contributions.
The CCB is payable from the month
(or arrival in Canada for immigrant children), but you only start receiving payments once your application is processed.

If your application is approved 3 months after your eligibility began, you will receive 3 months of retroactive payments in your first deposit. This can be a meaningful lump sum — be prepared for it and plan to put it toward an emergency fund or RESP.
Set up direct deposit with the CRA before applying so your payments arrive in your bank account immediately. You can set this up in CRA My Account under "Manage direct deposit."
Without direct deposit, the CRA sends cheques to your mailing address, which take additional time and can be lost or delayed.
Updating the CRA When Your Situation Changes
The CRA needs to know about the following changes:
— Update online or by calling the CRA
Child moves in or out of your home
— Handled at tax time; do not need to report mid-year

exampleScenarios.body: Preethi and her husband moved to Toronto as permanent residents in July with their 8-month-old son. She set up CRA My Account in October and applied for the CCB online, adding her son and uploading his Indian birth certificate (which was in English). The CRA processed the application in 6 weeks and sent her first payment of $672 in December — which included 3 months of retroactive payments from August.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Either parent can apply, but the CRA generally pays the CCB to the person who is primarily responsible for the child's care. If both parents share responsibility equally, either can apply — or in the case of separation, both parties should notify the CRA.
faqItems.question: Both my spouse and I are in Canada. Which one of us should apply?

faqItems.answer: The CRA will generally pay retroactive CCB going back 10 years from the date of your application, as long as you were eligible. File for any missed years as soon as possible.
faqItems.question: I forgot to apply for the CCB when my child arrived. How far back can I get retroactive payments?

faqItems.answer: Yes. CCB payments stop when the child turns 18. The final payment is for the month of the 18th birthday.
faqItems.question: My baby just turned 18 — do payments stop?

faqItems.answer: Temporary residents can apply for the CCB after living in Canada for 18 consecutive months and holding a valid permit for the 19th month. There is no waiting period for permanent residents or protected persons. *This article is for educational purposes only. For the most current information, visit canada.ca/child-benefit.*
faqItems.question: I am a temporary resident. Can I apply?

answerSummary: To qualify for a Canadian mortgage as a newcomer, you generally need a minimum 5% down payment, a credit score of 620–680+, at least 2 years of stable Canadian employment, and to pass the federal mortgage stress test. Some lenders offer newcomer mortgage programs with more flexible requirements. Understanding these requirements before you start house-hunting saves significant time and avoids disappointment.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Canadian residential mortgage rules are set by the federal government and the Office of the Superintendent of Financial Institutions (OSFI). The rules are designed to ensure borrowers can afford their mortgages even if interest rates rise.
— Sets the stress test requirement for federally regulated lenders
— Sets down payment rules by purchase price
Canada's down payment requirements are set by the federal government and depend on the purchase price:
Any purchase with less than 20% down requires
(also offered by private insurers Sagen and Canada Guaranty). This insurance protects the lender — not you — if you default.
Insurance premium rates (added to your mortgage):
Canada's mortgage stress test (OSFI Guideline B-20) requires that you qualify at
This means if your actual rate is 5.0%, you must prove you can afford payments at
The stress test reduces the mortgage amount you qualify for by approximately 20–22% compared to qualifying at your actual rate.
What Lenders Evaluate: The Five Cs
Lenders want 2+ years of Canadian credit history
No history of missed payments or collections in the past 2 years
Lenders use two ratios to assess affordability:
Housing costs = mortgage payment + property taxes + heating costs + 50% of condo fees
(Housing costs + all other debt payments ÷ Gross income) — must be ≤ 44%
Verified source of down payment — must be on deposit for at least 90 days or be a documented gift. Lenders will ask for 90 days of bank statements.
The lender will require a property appraisal. The mortgage cannot exceed the appraised value.
Your overall financial profile, employment stability, and relationship with the lender.
Required Documents for a Mortgage Application
2 years of T4 slips and Notice of Assessments
Letter of employment confirming title, salary, and tenure
90 days of bank statements (for down payment verification)
2–3 years of T1 General tax returns
Newcomers face two common barriers: limited Canadian credit history and limited Canadian employment history. Lenders address this in different ways.
Several major banks offer mortgage products specifically for new permanent residents:
Requires PR card, minimum 20% down, foreign credit assessment accepted, no Canadian credit history required
Allows purchase with as little as 5% down for recent permanent residents with a job offer or employment letter
Uses international credit history from select countries in lieu of Canadian history
Newcomer mortgages with flexible documentation requirements
Typical newcomer program requirements (vary by lender):
Minimum down payment: 5–20% depending on program
Proof of employment (Canadian job offer letter or 3–12 months of pay stubs)
Alternative credit documentation: international credit report, history of rent/utilities payments, bank account history

A mortgage broker accesses rates and products from multiple lenders and is often free to the borrower (paid by the lender on a successful mortgage). For newcomers, a broker who specializes in newcomer mortgages can significantly improve your options.
Closing Costs: What to Budget Beyond the Down Payment
First-time buyers often underestimate the additional costs of purchasing. Budget approximately
are eligible for a Land Transfer Tax rebate that offsets much or all of the land transfer tax. Check your provincial and municipal rules.

exampleScenarios.body: Raj arrived from India as a permanent resident in 2024 and started working immediately. By mid-2026, he had 2 years of Canadian employment, a 710 credit score, and $120,000 in savings ($80,000 for a down payment on a $650,000 condo, $30,000 for closing costs and reserves). He applied for a mortgage through TD's newcomer program. His GDS ratio was 36% and TDS was 42% — both within limits. At his actual rate of 5.25%, the stress test required him to qualify at 7.25%. His $80,000 down payment (12.3%) triggered CMHC insurance of $17,000, added to the mortgage. He was approved.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Yes — the Home Buyers' Plan allows first-time buyers to withdraw up to $60,000 from their RRSP tax-free for a home purchase. The withdrawal must be repaid over 15 years, or it is added to your income in equal installments.
faqItems.question: Can I use my RRSP for the down payment?

faqItems.answer: Yes. FHSA withdrawals for a first home purchase are completely tax-free and do not need to be repaid — making the FHSA even better than the RRSP HBP for this purpose.
faqItems.question: Can my FHSA also contribute to the down payment?

faqItems.answer: A fixed-rate mortgage locks in your interest rate for the mortgage term (typically 5 years in Canada). A variable rate fluctuates with the Bank of Canada's prime rate. Variable has historically been cheaper over time but creates uncertainty in monthly payments.
faqItems.question: What is a variable vs. fixed rate mortgage?

faqItems.answer: The mortgage term (when you renegotiate your rate) is typically 1–5 years. The amortization (total repayment period) is commonly 25 years for insured mortgages (the maximum for CMHC-insured loans) or up to 30 years for uninsured mortgages (as of 2024 rule changes allowing extended amortization for first-time buyers). *Sources: CMHC Homebuyer Guide 2025 (cmhc-schl.gc.ca); OSFI Guideline B-20; Department of Finance Canada; Financial Consumer Agency of Canada (FCAC). This article is for educational purposes only and does not constitute financial or mortgage advice.*

answerSummary: The Canada Child Benefit is a tax-free monthly payment to help families with the cost of raising children under 18. Payments range from approximately $650 to $7,787 per child per year depending on your income and the child's age. You can apply as soon as you arrive in Canada as a resident — you do not need to wait until tax season.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: What Is the Canada Child Benefit?

The CCB is one of Canada's most important social programs. It is a tax-free monthly payment sent directly to the primary caregiver of a child under 18, designed to help with the cost of raising children.
Tax-free — you do not pay income tax on CCB payments
Means-tested — payments decrease as family income increases
Paid monthly, typically on the 20th of each month
Adjusted annually in July based on your previous year's tax return
amounts for families with net income below approximately $36,502. Payments are reduced as income rises.
Rough estimate by income (1 child under 6):
To receive the CCB, you must:
and be primarily responsible for their care and upbringing
Be a Canadian citizen, permanent resident, or protected person
— OR a temporary resident who has lived in Canada for at least 18 months
Temporary residents (those on work permits or study permits) can qualify
of living in Canada, provided you have filed a tax return in Canada. Permanent residents and refugees can apply immediately upon arrival.
Option 1: When Registering the Child's Birth (Fastest)
If your child is born in Canada, you can apply for the CCB at the same time as registering the birth. The hospital will give you a form (RC66 — Canada Child Benefits Application) to submit.
Log into your CRA My Account and apply for child benefits under "Apply for child benefits." This is the fastest method if your child was born before you came to Canada.

Download form RC66 from the CRA website, complete it, and mail it to your nearest tax centre. Include proof of the child's birth (birth certificate or passport) and proof of your own immigration status.
Your SIN and the child's SIN (apply for the child's SIN at Service Canada)
Proof of the child's birth date (birth certificate, Canadian birth registration, or passport)
Your date of arrival in Canada
The CRA typically processes CCB applications within 8 weeks. Your first payment will include any retroactive amounts owed back to your eligibility start date.

The CCB is paid monthly, usually on the 20th of each month. It is deposited directly to your bank account if you have direct deposit set up with the CRA (strongly recommended — see our [CRA My Account guide](/articles/how-to-register-for-cra-my-account)).
The Annual Update: Why Filing Your Taxes Every Year Matters
The CCB is recalculated every July based on your previous year's tax return. If you do not file your taxes:
Your CCB payments will stop in July
You may owe back overpayments if the CRA cannot assess your income
This is one of the most important reasons every newcomer parent must file a tax return every year — even if they had very low or no income.
Most provinces offer their own additional child benefit on top of the federal CCB. These are usually calculated and paid automatically alongside the CCB if you are eligible. Examples:
You do not need to apply separately — your CCB application covers these as well.

exampleScenarios.body: Mei and her husband arrived from China as permanent residents in August with their 3-year-old daughter. They applied for the CCB online through CRA My Account in September. Their combined income in Canada that year was $58,000. The CRA sent their first CCB payment of $540/month in November (two months retroactive was also included). After their first full tax return, the CCB was recalculated to $510/month based on their full-year income assessment.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: The CCTB was replaced by the CCB in July 2016. The CCB is more generous for most families. If you have heard older references to the CCTB, they refer to the former program.
faqItems.question: Is the CCB the same as the Canada Child Tax Benefit (CCTB)?

faqItems.answer: No. The CCB requires the child to reside with you in Canada. If your children are still in your home country while you are in Canada, you are not eligible until they join you.
faqItems.question: My children are outside Canada — can I still receive CCB?

faqItems.answer: Your CCB amount is based on your own net income (not combined with a partner), which may result in a higher payment than two-parent households at the same total income.
faqItems.question: I am a single parent. Do I get more CCB?

faqItems.answer: Payments are recalculated each July based on the prior year's return. An income increase means a lower CCB in the following benefit year. You do not need to notify the CRA of income changes during the year — the recalculation happens automatically at tax time. *This article is for educational purposes only. For the most current CCB amounts and eligibility rules, visit canada.ca/child-benefit.*
faqItems.question: What happens to my CCB if my income increases significantly?

answerSummary: Canada is in the middle of a major national childcare expansion aimed at reaching $10/day regulated childcare for all families by 2026. In provinces like Quebec, this already exists. In others, regulated childcare fees are dropping but waitlists are long. Newcomer families can access the same childcare programs as other Canadians and can deduct childcare costs on their tax return, reducing their overall tax bill.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Canada's Childcare System: What Newcomers Should Know

Childcare in Canada is provincially administered. The federal government launched its Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care (CWELCC) system in 2021, with a goal of reducing average regulated childcare fees to $10/day for children under 6 by 2026. Progress varies significantly by province.
has had subsidized childcare ($10–$15/day) for decades through its Childcare Centres (CPEs) — but waitlists can span years
are progressively reducing regulated centre fees under CWELCC agreements
is a critical distinction — regulated centres meet provincial licensing, staffing, and safety standards; unregulated care (home daycares, nannies) does not have the same oversight
Regulated facilities operated by non-profit or commercial operators. Must meet provincial standards for:

A regulated home daycare provider cares for a small number of children (typically 3–6) in their private home. Must be licensed by the province. Lower cost than centre care, more home-like environment.

A caregiver looks after children in their home without provincial licensing. Lower cost but no guaranteed oversight of qualifications, safety standards, or staff ratios. Not eligible for subsidized fees under CWELCC in most provinces.
A nanny or au pair cares for children in your own home.
Often come through immigration pathways (e.g., the Home Child Care Provider Pilot). Their accommodation and board are considered partial compensation.
Hired privately. You are considered their employer and must deduct CPP, EI, and income tax from their wages, remit these to the CRA, and provide T4 slips.
$20–$30+/hour for live-out nannies in major cities.
Part-time programs (typically 2–4 hours per day) for children aged 2.5–4. Lower cost than full-day care, not a substitute for full-time childcare for working parents.
For school-age children (JK onwards), many schools and community organizations offer licensed before-and-after school programs. Fees vary widely and subsidies are available in some provinces.
Childcare waitlists in Canada's major cities can be 1–3 years long for popular licensed centres. As a newcomer,
put your name on waitlists as soon as possible — even before you arrive, if you can
How to Find and Join Waitlists
Use the Ontario Child Care Registry (formerly OneList) at childcare.ontario.ca for a centralized waitlist
Search through the provincial government's childcare finder tool or contact centres directly
— A national directory of licensed childcare with a map
Tips for Navigating Waitlists as a Newcomer
simultaneously — you can decline an offer if a preferred spot becomes available
Be flexible about location and start date — centres with less convenient locations often have shorter waits
Ask about infant spots specifically — these are often shorter-waited than toddler spots
Join local parent Facebook groups — families regularly post when they are giving up a spot
Canada-Wide Early Learning and Child Care (CWELCC)

Under CWELCC agreements, participating regulated centres charge reduced fees automatically. You do not need to apply separately — the reduction applies at enrolled centres that have joined the program.
Most provinces have additional subsidies for lower-income families, reducing costs further or even to zero. Requirements vary:
The fee subsidy program through local municipalities. Income-tested. Apply through your local Children's Services office.
ChildcareBC fee reduction and subsidy. Income-tested. Apply through the BC government's online portal.
All families pay the same low rate at CPEs — subsidy is universal.
One of the most important — and underused — tax benefits for parents in Canada is the
You can deduct eligible childcare costs from your income, which reduces your taxable income and may generate a larger refund.
The lower-income spouse must claim the deduction. If both partners earn income, the lower earner claims the deduction.
Licensed daycare or childcare centre fees
Nanny wages (you must report the nanny as your employee and provide a SIN)
Day camps and day sports schools (during school vacation)
Boarding schools and overnight camps (with limits)
Clothing, food, transportation, medical care separate from childcare.

A family with two children under 7 paid $16,000 in daycare in 2025. The lower-income parent earning $55,000 claims the full $16,000 deduction. At a combined marginal rate of ~31.5%, this saves approximately

exampleScenarios.body: Isabel and her husband arrived in Toronto from Brazil with their 14-month-old daughter. They immediately put their daughter on waitlists at four licensed daycare centres near their neighbourhood. Eight months later, a spot opened at a centre enrolled in Ontario's CWELCC program. Their daily fee was $32/day — down from the pre-subsidy $55/day. Isabel, as the lower earner, claimed their annual $8,320 in daycare fees as a deduction on her return, reducing her taxable income and generating an additional $1,600 in tax savings.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Yes, if the provider is over 18 years old and not the child's parent or a family member. You need the provider's SIN and name for your tax claim, and the amount must be reasonable. However, unlicensed providers are not covered by CWELCC fee reductions.
faqItems.question: Can I claim childcare expenses if I use an unlicensed home daycare?

faqItems.answer: Many families use a combination: unlicensed home daycare or a nanny while waiting for a licensed centre spot. Some provinces have emergency childcare bursaries for low-income families without access to licensed spaces.
faqItems.question: My child is on a waitlist. What can I do in the meantime?

faqItems.answer: Daycares provide full-day care (typically 7:00 AM–6:00 PM) for children from infancy through school age. Preschools/nursery schools operate part-time (2–4 hours) and focus on learning readiness for children approaching JK age. Most working parents need daycare, not preschool, for their primary care arrangement.
faqItems.question: What is the difference between a daycare and a preschool?

faqItems.answer: Some employer benefits plans include a Dependent Care FSA or DCSA. These allow pre-tax payroll contributions toward eligible childcare costs. Check with your HR department. Note that costs covered by your employer's plan cannot also be claimed as a tax deduction. *Sources: Employment and Social Development Canada (esdc.gc.ca/childcare); CRA T1 Line 21400 — Child Care Expenses; provincial childcare fee schedules (Ontario, BC, Quebec, Alberta) 2025. This article is for educational purposes only.*
faqItems.question: Can I use my FSA or benefits plan for childcare costs?

answerSummary: Canada's 10 provinces and 3 territories differ significantly in their tax rates, healthcare systems, immigration programs, cost of living, job markets, and quality of life. Ontario and British Columbia attract the most newcomers but carry the highest costs. Alberta has no provincial income tax on lower incomes and no sales tax. Quebec has the lowest-cost childcare and housing in major cities but the highest combined tax rates. This guide compares the key factors for the provinces where most newcomers settle.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Why Province Matters as Much as Country
When you choose Canada, you are also choosing a province — and that choice has major financial and lifestyle implications:
— The difference in after-tax income between the highest and lowest-taxed provinces can be $3,000–$8,000+ per year on the same salary
— Waiting periods, what's covered, and how to enroll are all provincial
— Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) offer pathways to PR tied to specific provinces
— Quebec's subsidized system is transformational for families
— Each province has a different job market by industry

Canada's financial, technology, and professional services hub. Toronto is the primary landing point for skilled immigrants. Strong job markets in finance, technology, healthcare, manufacturing, and government (Ottawa).
3-month waiting period for new residents. Covers doctor visits, hospital care. Does not cover prescription drugs, dental, or vision.
Actively reducing fees under CWELCC — average daycare fees have dropped significantly since 2021, but spots are scarce in high-demand areas. Waitlists of 1–3 years are common in Toronto.
Among the least affordable major cities in the world. Average home price ~$1.1M (2025). Rents are high. Suburbs and smaller Ontario cities offer much better value.
Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP) — streams for skilled workers, international students, and employer job offer holders.
Professional careers in finance, technology, law, healthcare. Large multicultural communities for most nationalities.
Technology (Vancouver is a major tech hub), natural resources, tourism, film industry, international trade (Pacific port).
12% (5% federal GST + 7% PST). Note: PST does not apply to most groceries or children's clothing.
— coverage starts immediately upon enrollment. This is a significant advantage for newcomers.
Under active CWELCC expansion. Average licensed infant fees around $20/day in 2025, down from $35+ before the program. Still competitive for spots.

Comparable to Toronto in cost — average home price ~$1.2M in Metro Vancouver. Rents are among Canada's highest. Many newcomers settle in Surrey, Burnaby, or the Fraser Valley for better affordability.
BC Provincial Nominee Program (BC PNP) — draws skilled workers and international graduates from BC institutions. Also a major destination for federal Express Entry draws.
Technology workers, those who value mild weather and outdoor lifestyle, families who qualify for BC's immediate healthcare coverage.
Energy sector (oil and gas), agriculture, technology (growing rapidly in Calgary), government and education (Edmonton). Calgary is increasingly a tech hub with significant US company offices.
lowest provincial income tax in Canada
10% provincial rate on all income up to $148,269
Albertans pay only the federal 5% GST — the lowest consumption tax in Canada.
— coverage starts immediately for new residents. Residents only pay the federal portion of CPP/EI, and Alberta's low income tax significantly increases take-home pay.
The federal CWELCC program applies — fees are being reduced. Alberta has also implemented its own fee subsidies. Average fees are lower than Ontario or BC.
Calgary and Edmonton are significantly more affordable than Toronto or Vancouver. Average Calgary home price ~$600K (2025); Edmonton ~$430K. Rents are moderate.
Alberta Advantage Immigration Program (AAIP) — multiple streams for skilled workers, rural renewal, and employer-sponsored workers.

Professionals in energy, construction, technology, agriculture. Families who want the lowest tax burden and more affordable homeownership. Those who prefer a car-based lifestyle (cities are spread out).
Alberta's economy is tied to energy prices, creating more economic volatility than other provinces. Winters in Edmonton are severe.
Aerospace, technology, video games (Montreal is a global gaming hub), pharmaceutical, culture and arts. Strong bilingual professional market in Montreal.
highest combined income tax in Canada
Quebec administers its own tax system — residents file
French is the official language of Quebec.

Law 101 (Charter of the French Language) requires businesses with 50+ employees to operate in French. Most professional jobs in Montreal require French proficiency. English communities exist in certain Montreal neighbourhoods.
3-month waiting period. Quebec runs its own separate provincial health plan.
Quebec's Centre de la petite enfance (CPE) system provides subsidized childcare at
— the most affordable in Canada by far. Waitlists exist but the cost savings versus other provinces are enormous for families with young children.
Among the most affordable major cities in Canada. Average 1-bedroom rent ~$1,400–$1,800/month; home prices ~$500K–$700K for Montreal metro.

Quebec controls its own immigration selection through the Quebec Skilled Worker Program (QSWP). Requires French language proficiency. The Certificat de sélection du Québec (CSQ) is required before applying for federal PR.
Francophone newcomers, families with young children (daycare savings can offset higher taxes), those in aerospace or gaming industries, those seeking a more European cultural environment.
Healthcare, education, government, ocean industries (fishing, offshore energy), tourism. Halifax has a growing technology sector.
Moderate — combined federal + Nova Scotia rate on $57K–$80K: ~35.1%.
3-month waiting period. Nova Scotia Health Card.
More affordable than Toronto/Vancouver. Halifax average rent (1BR): $1,600–$1,900.

Nova Scotia Nominee Program (NSNP). Also benefits from the Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP) — a federal pathway for skilled workers and international graduates who have a job offer in Atlantic Canada (NS, NB, PEI, Newfoundland).
Healthcare professionals, those in ocean industries, those seeking a smaller-city quality of life at lower cost, families who want a strong sense of community.
Agriculture, manufacturing, healthcare, education. Growing technology sector in Winnipeg.
12% (5% GST + 7% PST, though PST has partial exemptions on essentials)
Winnipeg is one of Canada's most affordable cities for both renting and buying.

Manitoba Provincial Nominee Program (MPNP) — very active program with streams for skilled workers and international student graduates. Manitoba has particularly strong infrastructure for Filipino and Ukrainian newcomer communities.
Those seeking very affordable living, agricultural or healthcare careers, strong cultural community networks.

exampleScenarios.body:
The Suarez family — two adults, both professionals, and two children under 5 — compared their net financial position between Ontario (Toronto) and Quebec (Montreal) for the same combined income of $150,000:
- Ontario: Combined income tax ~$35,000/year. Daycare for two children: ~$36,000/year. Housing (rent): ~$36,000/year. Total key costs: ~$107,000/year.
- Quebec: Combined income tax ~$43,000/year. Daycare for two children at CPE: ~$7,500/year. Housing (rent): ~$24,000/year. Total key costs: ~$74,500/year.
Despite paying significantly more in income tax, the family would have approximately $32,500 more per year in Montreal due to childcare and housing savings — money that could be directed to their RESP, FHSA, and TFSA.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Yes — Canadians and permanent residents are free to move between provinces. You simply update your address with the CRA, re-enroll in the new province's health plan, and update your driver's licence.
faqItems.question: Can I move from one province to another after arriving?

faqItems.answer: Federal immigration (Express Entry PR, family class) allows you to settle anywhere. Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs) require you to intend to settle in the nominating province, and you are expected to do so — though there is no legal mechanism preventing future movement.
faqItems.question: Does my immigration status affect which province I can settle in?

faqItems.answer: You are expected to settle in the nominating province, and this is assessed at the application stage. However, after receiving PR, you have the constitutional right to move anywhere in Canada. *Sources: IRCC Provincial Nominee Programs (ircc.canada.ca/pnp); CRA Provincial Tax Rates 2025; Statistics Canada; Conference Board of Canada Provincial Outlook 2025. This article is for educational and informational purposes only — immigration decisions should be made with input from a qualified immigration professional.*
faqItems.question: If I am nominated by a province through a PNP, do I have to stay there?

answerSummary: Canada's cost of living ranges dramatically by city. Toronto and Vancouver cost significantly more than most other Canadian cities — particularly for housing. Cities like Calgary, Edmonton, Ottawa, and Halifax offer a better balance of urban amenities and affordability. Understanding the true cost before choosing where to settle can save tens of thousands of dollars per year.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Why Where You Live Matters More Than the Salary

A $90,000 salary in Toronto and a $75,000 salary in Edmonton may leave you with similar or more disposable income in Edmonton once rent, taxes, and cost of living differences are factored in. Housing alone often swings the calculation by $10,000–$20,000/year.
This is why comparing cities is one of the most financially important decisions newcomers make.
The following estimates are for a
renting a 1-bedroom apartment, using public transit, and cooking most meals at home. All figures are approximate.
Note: BC's MSP (healthcare) has no premium — coverage is immediate for newcomers.
Alberta has no provincial income tax bracket below 10% and
(PST). Residents pay only the federal 5% GST on purchases.
Edmonton is consistently one of the most affordable major cities in Canada for housing while still offering a large-city job market.
Ottawa offers a strong government job market, bilingual environment, and notably lower housing costs than Toronto.
Montreal has low housing costs and excellent culture, but Quebec's income taxes are the highest in Canada — reducing take-home pay significantly versus other provinces.

Halifax has seen strong population growth from immigration and rising rents (up significantly from 2020 levels), but remains more affordable than Toronto and Vancouver. Nova Scotia offers Atlantic Immigration Pathway programs for newcomers.

Winnipeg is one of Canada's most affordable cities. It has a large Filipno, South Asian, and Ukrainian communities and has been a growing destination for newcomers via the Manitoba Provincial Nominee Program (MPNP).
Beyond the Numbers: Quality of Life Factors
Cost is only one dimension. When choosing where to settle, also consider:
— Technology and finance are strongest in Toronto and Vancouver; government in Ottawa; oil and gas in Calgary/Edmonton
— Large established communities of your cultural background exist in all major cities but vary in concentration
— Winters in Winnipeg and Edmonton are significantly harsher than Vancouver or Halifax
— Quebec requires French proficiency for most roles; bilingualism (French/English) is valued in Ottawa
— Some provinces offer immigration pathways tied to settling in that province (e.g., MPNP, Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program, BC PNP)
— If buying a home is a 3–5 year goal, mid-sized cities may make that goal achievable much sooner

exampleScenarios.body:
Leo had job offers in both Toronto ($95,000) and Calgary ($82,000). He used a cost-of-living calculator to compare:
- Toronto take-home (after federal + Ontario tax): ~$67,000
- Calgary take-home (after federal + Alberta tax): ~$60,000
The income gap after taxes was $7,000/year. But Calgary's lower rent saved him $8,400/year ($700/month less). Combined with lower transit costs and no PST on purchases, Leo's disposable income was actually higher in Calgary by approximately $3,000–$4,000/year — despite the lower nominal salary.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Canada is expensive relative to most of the world, especially for housing in major cities. It is comparable to or cheaper than Australia, the UK, and Switzerland for similar urban markets. Compared to the US, Canada's major cities are roughly comparable to New York or San Francisco in cost, while mid-sized Canadian cities are often more affordable than their American equivalents.
faqItems.question: Is Canada expensive compared to other countries?

faqItems.answer: HST (Harmonized Sales Tax) combines federal GST (5%) and provincial sales tax into one tax — used in Ontario, New Brunswick, Newfoundland, Nova Scotia, and PEI. PST (Provincial Sales Tax) is applied separately in BC, Saskatchewan, and Manitoba. Alberta has no PST. Quebec has its own QST.
faqItems.question: What is HST and PST?

faqItems.answer: Yes, modestly. Grocery prices are highest in the north and in smaller cities with fewer competitive retailers. In major metro areas, prices are similar, though produce can be cheaper in Vancouver due to proximity to BC's agricultural regions. *Sources: Statistics Canada Consumer Price Index (Regional, 2024); CMHC Rental Market Report 2025; Numbeo Cost of Living Data Canada 2025. This article is for informational purposes — actual costs vary by individual circumstances.*

answerSummary: Every Canadian employee sees two mandatory deductions on their paycheque beyond income tax: CPP (Canada Pension Plan) and EI (Employment Insurance). CPP is a compulsory retirement savings program — you contribute during your working years and receive a monthly pension at retirement. EI is an insurance program that pays you income replacement if you lose your job, go on parental leave, or face certain other life events. Both are tied to your employment and are not optional.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: When you look at a Canadian pay stub, you will see:
— your total wages before any deductions
CPP and EI are not taxes in the traditional sense — they are contributions to programs that directly benefit you. Understanding them helps you plan your finances and know what protection you have.
The Canada Pension Plan is a mandatory, contributory pension plan administered by the federal government. It provides:
— A monthly payment starting as early as age 60 (reduced) or as late as age 70 (enhanced), with age 65 being the standard start date
— If you become severely disabled before retirement age
— For a spouse or common-law partner after death
— For dependent children of disabled or deceased contributors
Your employer contributes an equal amount on your behalf — so the total contributed to your pension each year is approximately double your personal deduction.

Starting in 2024, a second tier of CPP contributions (CPP2) applies to earnings between the YMPE ($73,200) and the Year's Additional Maximum Pensionable Earnings (YAMPE, $81,900 for 2025). The rate is 4%. This affects higher earners and builds an additional, enhanced benefit on top of the base CPP.
How Much CPP Will You Receive at Retirement?
Your CPP retirement benefit depends on:
How long you contributed to CPP
How much you contributed (tied to your earnings)
The maximum CPP retirement pension at age 65 in 2025 is
($16,375/year). However, most Canadians receive less because they did not contribute at the maximum level for all eligible years.
As a newcomer, your CPP benefit will initially be lower than a Canadian who contributed for their entire working life. However, every year you work in Canada counts toward your eventual benefit.

Canada has social security agreements with over 50 countries. These agreements may allow you to combine your Canadian CPP contributions with contributions to your home country's pension system to qualify for benefits you would not reach individually. Check if your home country has an agreement with Canada at canada.ca/social-security-agreements.
When Can You Start Collecting CPP?
Employment Insurance (EI) is a federal insurance program that provides temporary income replacement to Canadians who:
Lose their job through no fault of their own (laid off, company closes)
Are ill, injured, or in quarantine and cannot work
Are pregnant, have just given birth, or are caring for a newborn or newly adopted child (parental benefits)
Are caring for a gravely ill family member (caregiving benefits)
Who Is Eligible to Receive EI Benefits?
To qualify for regular EI benefits (job loss), you must:
Have paid into EI (this happens automatically as soon as you work in Canada)
in the past 52 weeks — the required number of hours ranges from 420 to 700 hours depending on your region's unemployment rate
— voluntary resignation generally does not qualify unless you had "just cause"
You must have accumulated enough insurable hours in Canada. If you just started working in Canada, you may not yet meet the minimum hours threshold.
55% of your average insurable weekly earnings
, up to a maximum weekly benefit of
If you earned $60,000 annually ($1,154/week), your EI benefit would be 55% × $1,154 =
, depending on the regional unemployment rate and how many insurable hours you accumulated.
— 15 weeks at 55% of earnings, for the birth parent before and after birth
— 40 weeks at 55% of earnings, shareable between parents
— 69 weeks at 33% of earnings, shareable between parents (lower weekly amount but longer duration)
For newcomers starting families in Canada, parental EI benefits can be significant. You must have worked
in the past 52 weeks to qualify.

exampleScenarios.body:
James moved from Nigeria to Calgary and started a job earning $75,000/year. His first monthly pay stub showed:
- Gross: $6,250.00
- CPP deduction: ~$335.30 (monthly share of annual max)
- EI deduction: ~$89.79
- Federal + Alberta income tax: ~$1,280.00
- Net pay: ~$4,545
In November of his second year, James was laid off due to company downsizing. He immediately filed for EI through Service Canada online. He had accumulated over 700 insurable hours and qualified for $695/week (the maximum) for 36 weeks while he searched for a new role.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: Self-employed Canadians must pay **both the employee and employer share of CPP** (11.9% up to the maximum), which they claim on their T1 return. For EI, self-employed individuals are not required to pay premiums but can opt in voluntarily to access special benefits (maternity, parental, illness). You cannot access regular EI (job loss) as a self-employed person.
faqItems.question: I am self-employed. Do I pay CPP and EI?

faqItems.answer: No. Your CPP contributions remain on your record. If Canada has a social security agreement with your destination country, you may be able to combine contribution periods. If not, you can still receive a CPP pension when you reach eligible age, paid internationally.
faqItems.question: If I leave Canada, do I lose my CPP contributions?

faqItems.answer: EI requires a minimum number of insurable hours (420–700 depending on region). If you have not accumulated enough hours in Canada yet, you would not qualify for regular EI benefits. This is another reason to ensure your employment record starts accurately from your first day.
faqItems.question: My employer takes CPP and EI but I just arrived. What if I need EI soon?

faqItems.answer: If you over-contributed (e.g., you had multiple jobs and the combined CPP deductions exceeded the annual maximum), you receive the excess back as a refund when you file your tax return. The CRA calculates this automatically. *Sources: Employment and Social Development Canada (esdc.gc.ca); Canada Revenue Agency T4032 Payroll Deductions Tables (2025); Service Canada EI Program. This article is for educational purposes only.*
faqItems.question: Can I get a refund on CPP or EI premiums?

answerSummary: Canada's rental market is competitive, especially in Toronto and Vancouver. Average rents for a one-bedroom apartment in major cities range from $1,700 to $2,800/month. As a newcomer with no Canadian credit history, you will need to present alternative proof of your reliability — typically a larger deposit, employment letters, and international reference letters. Smaller cities, outer suburbs, and non-Toronto/Vancouver markets offer significantly better affordability.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The Canadian Rental Market: A Realistic Picture

Canada's housing affordability challenge is well-documented. The Canada Mortgage and Housing Corporation (CMHC) reports a national rental vacancy rate that has been below 2% in most major markets — meaning available units are quickly claimed. This creates a competitive market where prospective tenants often compete for the same listing.
Average monthly rents, one-bedroom apartment (2025 estimates):
— Canada's largest dedicated rental listing site
— Very widely used for private landlord listings (more risk of scams — see below)
— Aggregates listings across platforms with a map view
— Declining use but still present in some cities
— Organizations like COSTI (Toronto), MOSAIC (Vancouver), or local Immigrant Services Societies often maintain housing resources and referrals for newcomers
— Many cities have newcomer community Facebook groups where housing leads are shared
— Often have informal housing networks for members of specific communities
In a competitive market, being prepared with documentation gives you a significant advantage.
— Employment letter on company letterhead stating your job title, start date, and annual salary
— Last 1–2 pay stubs (if you have started work)
— Previous landlord references (international references are accepted, provide contact info)
For Newcomers Without Canadian Credit History
Without a Canadian credit score, landlords have less data to assess your reliability. You can compensate by offering:
First and last month's rent upfront
(standard in Ontario; ask in other provinces)
— A bank statement showing you have 3–6 months of rent in savings
Reference letter from a previous landlord
in your home country (with contact details for verification)
— If you have a Canadian friend, colleague, or family member willing to co-sign
Canada's rental laws are provincial. Key protections that exist in most provinces:

Landlords can only raise rent once per year, by a maximum percentage set by the provincial government (the "rent increase guideline" — 2.5% for 2025). Exempt: units first occupied after November 2018.
Annual increases limited to the CPI (inflation rate) — capped at 3% for 2025.
No province-wide rent control. Landlords can increase rent once every 365 days with 3 months' written notice.
Increases must be "reasonable"; tenants can contest increases at the Tribunal administratif du logement (TAL).

Landlords cannot evict you without cause. Valid reasons include non-payment of rent, damage to the property, or the landlord requiring the unit for personal use. The process requires formal written notice and, in most provinces, access to a tribunal before eviction can proceed.
Landlords are legally required to keep rental units in good repair. Tenants have the right to a unit that:
Is structurally sound with working heat, plumbing, and electricity
Meets local health and safety standards
Is maintained in a state of good repair
If a landlord fails to make necessary repairs, tenants can file a complaint with the provincial landlord-tenant tribunal.
Lease Agreements: What to Review Before Signing
Fixed-term (e.g., 1 year — you commit to that period) vs. month-to-month (more flexible, often more expensive)
Hydro (electricity), heat, water, parking, and laundry are sometimes included, sometimes not
to end the tenancy (typically 60 days in most provinces)
Whether you can sublet if you need to leave early
Ontario, British Columbia, and several other provinces have

that landlords must use. These protect tenants by ensuring key terms are disclosed. If a landlord refuses to use the standard form, tenants in Ontario have the right to withhold the last month's rent until provided with it.
Rental Scams: Red Flags for Newcomers
Newcomers are disproportionately targeted by rental fraud. Common scams:
— A beautiful unit listed significantly below market rent
and wants e-transfer or Western Union deposit before you view the unit
— Scammers often use non-traceable phone numbers
Requests to pay via cryptocurrency, gift cards, or wire transfer

Never pay any deposit before viewing a unit in person (or via a live video call if you are in another city) and confirming the landlord has the legal right to rent it (ask for proof of ownership or authorization from the owner).

Canada has a limited supply of non-market (subsidized) housing. Waitlists for social housing are typically very long (2–10+ years in major cities) and are generally not a viable option for recent newcomers. However, if you have a very low income, registering on the waitlist early makes sense.
Some provinces offer rent supplement programs:
Ontario Works (social assistance) includes a shelter allowance
Rental Assistance Program (RAP) for low-income families
A top-up to provincial/territorial housing assistance — combined with provincial programs for eligible low-income renters
Contact your provincial Ministry of Housing or local settlement agency for current availability and eligibility.

exampleScenarios.body: Nadia and her husband arrived from Morocco in February with two children. They needed a 3-bedroom apartment in Ottawa. Their budget was $2,200/month. They registered with the local settlement agency, which connected them with a housing navigator. Within 10 days, they had viewed 4 units and signed a lease on a 3-bedroom townhouse at $2,100/month in the suburbs. Because they had no Canadian credit history, they provided: a letter from Nadia's employer, proof of $15,000 in savings, and a reference from their previous Casablanca landlord (translated). The landlord accepted first and last month's rent and approved the application.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: In Canada, landlords cannot discriminate based on national or ethnic origin, place of origin, or citizenship — these are protected grounds under the Canadian Human Rights Act and provincial human rights codes. However, landlords can decline applicants based on insufficient income or lack of credit history, which disproportionately affects newcomers in practice.
faqItems.question: Can a landlord refuse to rent to me because I am a newcomer?

faqItems.answer: Landlords often run a credit check to confirm you have a history of paying obligations. As a newcomer with no Canadian credit, the check may return "no file found" rather than a bad score. Supplement this by providing income proof and references proactively.
faqItems.question: What is a "credit check" in the rental context?

faqItems.answer: Rules vary by province. In Ontario, landlords are limited to collecting first and last month's rent — no separate damage deposit is permitted. In BC and Alberta, damage deposits of up to half a month's rent are standard. Always get a receipt. *Sources: CMHC Rental Market Report 2025 (cmhc-schl.gc.ca); provincial Residential Tenancies Acts (Ontario, BC, Alberta, Quebec). This article is for educational purposes only and does not constitute legal advice.*
faqItems.question: How much should I budget for a damage deposit?

answerSummary: Your Social Insurance Number (SIN) is a 9-digit number issued by the federal government. You need it to work legally in Canada, file taxes, and access government benefits. Most newcomers can apply at a Service Canada office the same week they arrive — bring your immigration documents and expect a 15–30 minute visit.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: What Is a SIN and Why Do You Need It?

A Social Insurance Number (SIN) is Canada's way of tracking your employment income and tax contributions. Think of it like a Social Security Number in the United States or a National Insurance Number in the United Kingdom.
You will need your SIN to:
Start a job legally in Canada
File your income tax return with the CRA
Open certain bank accounts and investment accounts (RRSP, TFSA, FHSA)
Apply for government benefits like the Canada Child Benefit (CCB) and Employment Insurance (EI)
You should apply for your SIN as soon as possible after arriving.
Without it, your employer cannot process your first paycheque properly, and you may lose out on tax refunds.
Who Is Eligible to Get a SIN?
You can apply for a SIN if you are:
A temporary resident with a valid work permit
A refugee claimant with authorization to work
If you are here on a study permit and do not have a work permit, you may still get a SIN if your permit includes a condition that allows you to work on- or off-campus.
If your immigration status is temporary (e.g., work permit), your SIN will begin with a
and will have an expiry date linked to your permit. You must renew it when your permit is renewed.
proving your identity and immigration status:
If your primary document does not show your current address in Canada, bring a secondary document such as a utility bill, lease agreement, or bank statement.
How to Apply for a SIN
Option 1: Apply in Person at Service Canada (Recommended)
This is the fastest option. You will receive your SIN the same day in most cases.

Find your nearest Service Canada Centre at [canada.ca/en/employment-social-development/corporate/portfolio/service-canada.html](https://www.canada.ca/en/employment-social-development/corporate/portfolio/service-canada.html)
Tell the agent you are applying for a SIN
They will issue your SIN verbally or on a confirmation letter —
there is no physical SIN card anymore
Wait times vary. Arriving when the office opens (typically 8:30 AM) reduces waiting.
Service Canada has an online application portal for eligible newcomers.
Upload clear photos of your documents
The online process is convenient but slower. If you need to start work quickly, in-person is better.
Mail applications are available but take up to
and carry a risk of your original documents being lost in transit. This option is not recommended unless you are in a remote area with no nearby Service Canada office.
Your SIN is sensitive personal information. Misuse can lead to identity theft and tax fraud.
Do not carry your SIN confirmation letter
Store it in a secure place at home (a locked drawer or safe)
Only share your SIN when legally required (your employer, bank, or the CRA)
Merchants, landlords, and most businesses do
have a legal right to ask for your SIN
If you believe your SIN has been misused, contact Service Canada at 1-800-206-7218.
What If You Lose Your SIN Number?
There is no longer a physical SIN card. Your SIN is on file with the government. If you have forgotten your number, you can find it on:
Your T4 slip from a previous employer
Your Notice of Assessment from the CRA
To get an official confirmation letter, visit a Service Canada office in person with your ID.

exampleScenarios.body: Maria arrived in Toronto from Mexico on a Permanent Resident visa in January. On her third day in Canada, she took the subway to the nearest Service Canada office with her COPR letter and passport. She arrived at 9:00 AM, waited about 20 minutes, and left with her SIN number written on an official letter. That afternoon, she gave her new employer the number so her payroll could be set up correctly.
exampleScenarios.title: Example Scenario

faqItems.answer: No. Applying for a SIN is completely free. Be cautious of third-party websites that charge a fee to help you apply — the process is straightforward and free at Service Canada.
faqItems.question: Does getting a SIN cost anything?

faqItems.answer: You can start a job, but your employer is required by law to collect your SIN within 3 days of you starting work. You should apply before your first day if at all possible.
faqItems.question: Can I work without a SIN?

faqItems.answer: A SIN beginning with 9 is issued to temporary residents (work permit holders, etc.). It has an expiry date and must be renewed when your immigration status changes or your permit expires. When you become a permanent resident, apply for a new SIN that does not start with 9.
faqItems.question: My SIN starts with a 9. What does that mean?

faqItems.answer: No. You are only ever issued one SIN in your lifetime. If you are a permanent resident and previously had a temporary SIN (starting with 9), Service Canada will issue you a new number and cancel the old one. *This article is for educational purposes only. For the most current information, visit the official Service Canada website at canada.ca.*
faqItems.question: Can I have more than one SIN?

answerSummary: Canada has an extensive social safety net with dozens of benefit programs. Many are available to permanent residents from the moment they arrive; temporary residents have access to some benefits after a qualifying period. Most benefits require you to file an annual income tax return to receive them — this is the single most important action to unlock ongoing government support.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The Key to Unlocking Benefits: File Your Taxes
Before going through each benefit, there is one rule that applies to almost all of them:
File your income tax return every year, even if you have no income.

The CRA uses your filed return to determine eligibility for the vast majority of benefit programs. Many newcomers miss out on hundreds or thousands of dollars in annual payments simply because they did not realize they needed to file.
A quarterly, tax-free payment to help low- and middle-income Canadians offset the Goods and Services Tax (GST) and Harmonized Sales Tax (HST) they pay on purchases.
Canadian residents with low-to-moderate income. Includes permanent residents. Temporary residents may qualify.
File your tax return. The CRA automatically assesses eligibility.
Payments are made quarterly (January, April, July, October).
Monthly, tax-free payments for families raising children under 18.
Canadian residents (including PR holders). Temporary residents qualify after 18 months.
Apply through CRA My Account, at birth registration (for newborns), or via Form RC66.
See our full guide: [Canada Child Benefit Guide](/articles/canada-child-benefit-ccb-guide)
A refundable tax credit for low-income working Canadians and permanent residents. As of 2023, advance payments are sent automatically quarterly.
Working Canadians/PRs with low income. Must be 19+ or have a spouse/dependant. Single adults: net income under ~$35,000; families: net income under ~$60,000 (thresholds vary by province).
Claim Schedule 6 on your tax return. Tax software handles this automatically.
A new federal program (rolled out 2024–2025) providing dental coverage for Canadians without existing dental insurance and with a family income under $90,000.
Enrolled in phases — as of 2025, open to adults 18+ and families meeting the income threshold. Permanent residents and some temporary residents are eligible.
Basic dental (exams, cleanings, X-rays, fillings, extractions). The plan covers 60–100% of costs based on income.

answerSummary: Canada uses a graduated (progressive) income tax system. You pay a lower rate on your first dollars of income and higher rates only on income above each threshold — not on all your income at once. In addition to federal tax, each province charges its own income tax on top. Your total tax bill combines both layers, but the average effective rate most Canadians actually pay is much lower than their marginal (top bracket) rate.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Canada's Progressive Tax System: The Key Concept
The single most important thing to understand about Canadian income tax is this:
moving into a higher bracket only affects the portion of your income above that threshold.
It does not cause all your income to be taxed at the higher rate.

If you earn $60,000 in Ontario in 2025, you do not pay 26.5% (the combined bracket for $57,375–$100,392) on all $60,000. You pay 15% on the first $57,375 federally, and 26.5% only on the last $2,625 that sits above the threshold.
This is one of the most common misconceptions newcomers (and many long-time Canadians) have about the tax system.
Canada's federal government taxes income at graduated rates. The 2025 federal brackets are:
of $16,129 (federal, 2025) is deducted from your income. This means the first $16,129 of income is not taxed at all federally.
Provincial Income Tax: A Second Layer
Each province and territory charges its own income tax on top of the federal tax. Provincial rates and brackets vary significantly.
2025 Combined Marginal Rates at $80,000 Income (Federal + Provincial)
Quebec administers its own separate income tax system through Revenu Québec, not the CRA, which is why Quebec residents file two tax returns each year (one federal, one provincial).
Marginal Rate vs. Effective Rate: What You Actually Pay
The rate that applies to your next dollar of income (top bracket you are in).
Your total tax as a percentage of your total income.
For most Canadians, the effective rate is significantly lower than the marginal rate because lower brackets apply to the first portions of income.

— Each dollar contributed reduces taxable income by one dollar
Union dues and professional membership fees
(if moving for work or school, 40 km closer rule)

The result after these deductions is your

How Tax Is Collected: The Withholding System
In Canada, your employer deducts estimated income tax from each paycheque and remits it directly to the CRA. This is called
The amount withheld is based on your TD1 form — a form you fill out when you start a new job that tells your employer about your expected income and basic credits (such as the Basic Personal Amount).
When you file your annual tax return, the CRA reconciles:
Tax actually owed based on your full income picture
If more was withheld than you owe →

The TD1 Form: Setting Up Withholding Correctly
is a form you complete for your employer when you start a job. It tells your employer how much tax to deduct from each paycheque.

The default TD1 applies the Basic Personal Amount only. If you have additional credits (e.g., eligible tuition, disability amounts), claim them on the form to reduce withholding and improve your monthly cash flow.
CPP, EI, and Other Payroll Deductions
Before income tax, three other deductions appear on every paycheque. These are not income tax but are often confused with it:
A retirement contribution. Employee rate: 5.95% on earnings between $3,500 and $73,200 (2025). See our full guide: [CPP and EI Deductions Explained](/articles/cpp-ei-deductions-explained-newcomers).
An insurance premium. Employee rate: 1.64% on insurable earnings up to $65,700 (2025).
The graduated federal + provincial tax described in this article.
If you arrived in Canada partway through the year, you file as a
You report only Canadian income from the date you became a resident
Your Basic Personal Amount and most credits are prorated based on the number of days you were a resident
You may also need to report world income for the period before arrival if Canada has a tax treaty with your home country
explains part-year filing in detail. Tax software handles the proration automatically when you enter your arrival date.

exampleScenarios.body: Fatima arrived in Canada in July 2025 and worked for 6 months, earning $38,000. She completed a TD1 and TD1ON for her Ontario employer, claiming only the Basic Personal Amount. Her employer withheld approximately $5,100 in income tax. When she filed her return in March 2026, entering her July arrival date, the software prorated her Basic Personal Amount to 6/12 of the full year. Her actual federal + Ontario tax came to approximately $4,400 — resulting in a $700 refund. She also qualified for a partial GST/HST credit automatically.

answerSummary: CRA My Account is the Canada Revenue Agency's secure online portal. Once registered, you can view all your tax slips, check RRSP and TFSA contribution room, track benefit payments (GST credit, CCB), and manage your direct deposit information. Registration requires your SIN and either a previous tax return or a confirmed bank account.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: CRA My Account is your personal online hub for all things tax and benefits in Canada. Think of it like an online banking portal, but for your relationship with the federal government.
View all your T4, T5, and other tax slips filed by employers and financial institutions
Check your RRSP contribution room and TFSA contribution room
See your Notice of Assessment for past years
Track benefit payments (GST/HST credit, Canada Child Benefit)
Set up or change your direct deposit for tax refunds and benefits
File a tax return (via NETFILE-certified software linked to your account)
Request a change to a previously filed return
Authorize a tax representative (e.g., accountant) to access your account
A previous year's Canadian tax return
(the CRA will ask you to confirm a number from it), OR
mailed to your address (takes 5–10 business days)
and you have not filed a return yet, you will need to wait for the mailed security code option or file your first return and then register afterward.
Enter your SIN, date of birth, and postal code
If you have a previous return: enter a line item from it (e.g., Line 15000 — total income)
If you do not: request a security code by mail
(or link to a Sign-In Partner — see below)
If you bank with one of Canada's major banks (RBC, TD, Scotiabank, BMO, CIBC, Desjardins, etc.), you can use your
to sign into CRA My Account via the "Sign-In Partner" option. This skips the security code step and provides immediate access.
What to Do First After Registering
Once logged in, your immediate priorities as a newcomer:
— Go to "Manage direct deposit" and enter your Canadian bank account info. This ensures any refunds or benefit payments go directly to your account rather than arriving by cheque.
— Under "Notification preferences," sign up for email notifications so the CRA emails you when new correspondence is available. This prevents important notices from being missed in paper mail.
Check your TFSA and RRSP room
— Under "RRSP and savings plans," you will see your available contribution room for each account.
— Under "Tax information slips," you can see all slips on file, useful for cross-referencing with what your employer or bank sent you.

Double-check your SIN, date of birth, and current postal code. If you recently moved, the CRA may have your old address — use the postal code on file with the CRA (from your last return or immigration record).
Too many failed login attempts locks the account. Call CRA at 1-800-959-8281 to unlock.
Allow 10–15 business days. If it still has not arrived, call CRA to request a new one or try the Sign-In Partner option.

exampleScenarios.body: Omar arrived from Egypt in April and started work immediately. He applied for his SIN in his first week. In late September, he registered for CRA My Account using the Sign-In Partner option through his RBC online banking. Within minutes he was logged in. He set up direct deposit to his RBC chequing account, reviewed the single T4 on file (from a part-year job), and confirmed his TFSA contribution room for the year. When tax season arrived in March, his refund was deposited directly within 8 days of filing.

answerSummary: The First Home Savings Account (FHSA) is a relatively new registered account (launched in 2023) that helps first-time homebuyers save for a down payment. Like an RRSP, contributions are tax-deductible. Like a TFSA, qualifying withdrawals (to buy your first home) are completely tax-free. You can contribute up to $8,000 per year and $40,000 lifetime.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: The FHSA is the most tax-advantaged account in Canada for one very specific purpose: saving for your first home purchase. It combines the best feature of two other accounts:
Contributions reduce your taxable income (i.e., you get a tax deduction and potentially a refund)
Qualifying withdrawals to buy a first home are completely tax-free
If you are planning to buy a home in Canada within the next 1–15 years, the FHSA should be a priority.
To open an FHSA, you must:
(or 19 in provinces where the age of majority is 19)
— meaning you have not owned a qualifying home that you lived in at any point during the current calendar year, or during the preceding four years
Most newcomers qualify as first-time homebuyers because they did not own a home in Canada in the preceding four years.
If you open an FHSA in 2025 but only contribute $3,000, you can contribute up to $13,000 in 2026 ($8,000 for 2026 + $5,000 carried forward from 2025).
Tax Benefits: Both Coming In and Going Out
On the Way In — Tax Deduction
Like an RRSP, every dollar you contribute to an FHSA is deducted from your taxable income. If you contribute $8,000 and your marginal tax rate is 40%, your tax refund is approximately $3,200.
Unlike an RRSP, you can also carry forward unused deductions — so you do not have to claim the deduction in the same year you contribute.
On the Way Out — Tax-Free Withdrawal
, all withdrawals from the FHSA are completely tax-free: contributions, growth, and all. You pay zero tax.
This is the key advantage over the RRSP's Home Buyers' Plan — RRSP withdrawals via the HBP must be repaid over 15 years.
FHSA withdrawals for a first home never need to be repaid.
What If You Don't Buy a Home?

If you open an FHSA but never end up buying a home (or if you close the account after 15 years), you can transfer the balance to your RRSP or RRIF with no tax consequences. The balance is not lost — it simply becomes RRSP savings.
FHSA vs RRSP Home Buyers' Plan (HBP): Which Is Better?
— maxing out the FHSA first, then also using the HBP from their RRSP for additional funds.
All major Canadian banks and most investment platforms now offer FHSAs:
Open the account the same way you would a TFSA or RRSP — in person or online, with your SIN.

exampleScenarios.body:
Michael arrived from the Philippines in 2024 as a permanent resident. He knew he wanted to buy a condo in 3–5 years. He opened an FHSA at Questrade and contributed the maximum $8,000. At his marginal rate of 32%, this gave him a tax refund of approximately $2,560 which he reinvested in his TFSA. Over 5 years, contributing $8,000/year and earning 6% annually, his FHSA would grow to approximately $47,600 — and every dollar of that would come out completely tax-free when he used it toward his down payment.
Use our [FHSA Calculator](/tools/rrsp-refund) to model your own scenario.

faqItems.answer: The home must be a qualifying home (generally: a residential property in Canada you intend to occupy as your principal residence within 1 year of buying). This includes condos, detached houses, townhomes, and duplexes.

answerSummary: You can open a Canadian bank account within your first week of arriving, even before you have a job or credit history. All major banks offer special newcomer programs with reduced or waived fees for 1–2 years. You will need your passport, proof of immigration status, and a Canadian address.
author: Maple Insight

children.text: Why Opening a Bank Account Comes First
Before you do almost anything else financially in Canada, you need a bank account. You will need it to:
Receive your paycheque via direct deposit
Pay rent (most landlords require electronic transfer or a cheque)
Set up bill payments for phone, internet, and utilities
Apply for a credit card to start building your Canadian credit score
Receive government payments like the Canada Child Benefit
Without a Canadian bank account, even basic daily life becomes difficult. The good news: Canadian banks actively compete for newcomer customers and make it easy to get started.
Most banks require two pieces of identification, with at least one being a government-issued photo ID:
Credit card or bank card from your home country
You will also need to provide a
— even a temporary address such as a friend's home, a hotel, or a short-term rental is usually accepted.
You do not need a job, credit history, or a minimum deposit to open a basic chequing account in Canada. Banks are required by law to open a basic account for eligible customers.
Types of Accounts You Will Need
Your everyday account for spending, bill payments, and receiving your salary. Most newcomer programs offer fee-free chequing for the first year.
A place to set aside money. High-interest savings accounts (HISAs) at online banks often offer better interest rates than the big banks.
Once you have been a Canadian resident for at least a year and have filed a tax return, you should open a TFSA for investing or saving — you do not pay tax on growth inside it.
For now, focus on getting a chequing account open first. You can add other account types over time.
Newcomer Banking Programs at Canada's Major Banks
All of Canada's major banks offer some form of newcomer banking program. Features change regularly — always confirm current offers directly with the bank.
(e.g., Simplii Financial, Tangerine, EQ Bank) often have no monthly fees permanently and competitive savings rates, but may offer less newcomer-specific support.
Choose a bank based on location, language support, or program features
Book an appointment online or walk in during business hours
Bring your documents (passport + immigration document + Canadian address)
Ask about the newcomer program specifically and confirm the fee waiver period
Leave with a bank card (debit card) typically within 5–7 business days
Most banks now allow online account opening, but some require in-person verification for newcomers without an existing Canadian credit profile. If the online process stalls, visit a branch.
Comparing Accounts: What to Look For
When choosing a newcomer banking package, look beyond just the free-fee period:
Monthly fee after the promo period ends
Number of free transactions per month
— useful while you are getting settled
— many major banks offer service in Mandarin, Cantonese, Punjabi, Arabic, and other languages

exampleScenarios.body: Ahmed arrived from Egypt in September on a work permit. He visited a TD Bank branch near his new apartment with his passport, his work permit, and a copy of his lease agreement (for the address). The appointment took about 45 minutes. He left with a new chequing account, a debit card on the way in the mail, and a secured credit card application approved — all under the TD New to Canada package at no fee for the first year.
